bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as institutional accountability—emphasizing an "ethics probe," a coordinated "pressure campaign," and political consequences—while right-leaning pieces foreground lurid personal scandal, using sensational labels like "sex suicide scandal," calling Gonzales "embattled" and highlighting that the aide "fatally set herself on fire"; the left often names the staffer and the right amplifies partisan tags and graphic detail.

Pivotal dividing lines are the staffer’s identity, allegations of coercion and text-message evidence, and whether leadership pressure or moral spectacle drove the exit; center outlets de-emphasize lurid language, quote Gonzales's "mistake"/"lapse in judgment," and stick to chronology.
Both sides agree he admitted the affair, will finish his term and face an ethics inquiry, reflecting partisan priorities—left stressing institutional accountability, right moralizing scandal.
summary: Texas Republican Tony Gonzales announced he will not seek reelection after admitting to an affair with a former staff member who later died by suicide, though he plans to complete his current term in Congress.
The revelation triggered a House Ethics Committee investigation and intense pressure from Republican leadership and fellow lawmakers, some of whom have called for his resignation or punishment.
Anna Paulina Luna introduced resolutions to strip Gonzales of committee assignments and censure him, while Hakeem Jeffries said he would support expelling him from the House.

Although GOP leaders, including Mike Johnson, urged Gonzales to abandon his reelection bid, they stopped short of calling for his resignation because Republicans hold only a narrow majority in the House.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the 219-212 House rejection as Republicans "shot down" efforts to "rein in" what they call Trump's Iran war , emphasizing costs, U.S. troop casualties and about four Democrats defecting; right-leaning accounts portray the vote as a "greenlights" moment—letting "Operation Epic Fury" continue—with triumphant, permissive tones.

Rhetorically the left leans urgent , the right uses martial affirmations , while centrist coverage foregrounds legal questions—whether an "imminent" threat justified strikes—and de-emphasizes casualties and the operation name.
Both agree the close, party-line vote tests presidential strategy, underscoring splits over congressional oversight versus executive authority.
summary: On Thursday, the Republican-controlled House of Representatives voted down a bipartisan resolution directing President Donald Trump to end U.S. military operations in Iran, with the measure failing 219 to 212 one day after the Senate blocked a similar resolution.

Citing the Constitution and the 1973 War Powers Resolution, supporters argued Congress should reassert its authority, as Rep. Thomas Massie and Rep. Ro Khanna authored the bipartisan measure after Trump submitted the resolution on Tuesday.
Casualty figures underscore the stakes, as six Americans have been killed in the conflict so far, and observers say failed votes grant implicit authorization for U.S. strikes that began last weekend.
The House vote was widely described as symbolic, and Rep. Thomas Massie acknowledged the measure was headed for defeat, while some Republicans said the issue could return if the war expands.
Despite campaign rhetoric against new wars, critics said the resolution's failure reflected Republican deference to Trump, with House leadership pushing back earlier this week.
title: House rejects bid to curb Trump's war powers on Iran

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the scandal as a "MAGA" moral failure, using evocative language like "gruesome suicide" to underscore the tragedy.
Conversely, right-leaning coverage emphasizes internal discipline, portraying the call to "drop" the bid as a proactive stand for accountability.
A pivotal divide emerges over a "$300,000 shakedown" extortion claim, which the right highlights while the left de-emphasizes it to focus on misconduct.
Center-Leaning reports maintain neutrality by framing the affair as a "lapse in judgment.
Both sides agree on the Ethics Committee's investigation, reflecting a shared concern for institutional integrity despite clashing ideological interpretations.
summary: Published March 5, 2026, House Republican leaders urged Rep. Tony Gonzales, R‑Texas, to drop his reelection bid after he admitted Wednesday to an affair with a staffer who later died by suicide.
Regina Santos‑Aviles, a former staffer, reportedly had a relationship with Gonzales before her Sept. 15, 2025 death by suicide, after her husband discovered explicit May 2024 texts.
Gonzales told conservative podcaster Joe Pags `I had a lapse in judgement` and `When you make mistakes like this, you know, it's never easy`, while saying he will cooperate and not resign.

The House Committee on Ethics announced a formal investigation and created an Investigative Subcommittee to examine whether Gonzales violated the Code of Official Conduct or dispensed special favors, following allegations of sexual misconduct and discrimination. The investigation was announced Wednesday.
Gonzales is now headed into a May 26 runoff with Brandon Herrera after neither candidate topped 50%, with vote totals swinging from an early voting 45%–40% edge to Herrera's 49% and Gonzales's 36%.
title: House Republican leaders urge Rep. Gonzales to end reelection bid after he admitted to affair with aide

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the change as overdue accountability—emphasizing alleged deadly ICE outcomes, labels like "ICE Barbie" and "scandal‑plagued," and intense "grilling"—while right-leaning outlets foreground the president’s accusation that Noem "lied to Congress," amplify the $220M ad controversy, and hail her successor as a "MAGA warrior," portraying the move as decisive.

Pivotal dividing lines are deaths tied to ICE operations and Trump’s explicit allegation—each side elevates the one that supports its narrative, while procurement/no‑bid contract details are de-emphasized by conservatives; centrist reports focus on dates and confirmation process.
Both report the exit, nomination, and hearings, reflecting deeper ideological rifts over immigration, governance, and loyalty.
summary: On Thursday, President Donald Trump fired Department of Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem and said he will nominate Senator Markwayne Mullin, effective March 31, 2026, Trump wrote on Truth Social.

Pressure built following congressional hearings this week, where lawmakers questioned Noem about a $220 million government ad campaign and her removal followed criticism after two U.S. citizens died in Minneapolis, prompting the move.

Noem's tenure included aggressive enforcement in Los Angeles, Chicago and Washington, D.C., while deaths in immigration detention rose to a two-decade high and the White House denied her claim that the president approved the ad campaign.

Trump said he will nominate Senator Markwayne Mullin to replace Kristi Noem, who will serve as Special Envoy for The Shield of the Americas; Mullin can serve as acting secretary during the U.S. Senate confirmation process.
Mullin's background— including his Tribal ties and congressional service— makes him a 'MAGA Warrior' who could influence DHS policy on deportations, Trump said.
title: Trump says he will replace DHS Secretary Kristi Noem with Sen. Markwayne Mullin

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump’s statement as imperial overreach—using terms like "assassination," a "demand to intervene," and citing Iranian calls to "shed" his blood—while right-leaning pieces present it as legitimate U.S. influence, valorizing words such as "demands," "must be involved," and dismissing Mojtaba as "unacceptable" or a "lightweight.
The left leans on accusatory, moral language and escalation warnings; the right uses assertive, patriotic rhetoric normalizing intervention.
Dividing flashpoints include whether Khamenei’s death was an assassination, legal/international-law implications, and the Venezuela comparison; center coverage highlights diplomatic contradictions.
Both agree Trump claimed he must be involved and rejected Mojtaba amid regional violence.
summary: On March 5, President Donald Trump told Reuters the United States wants to help choose Iran's next leader and encouraged Iranian Kurdish opposition groups to go on the offensive.

After Khamenei's killing on Feb. 28, Iran formed a temporary leadership council including President Masoud Pezeshkian, while the Assembly of Experts will select a new supreme leader; U.S. strikes continued with Trump overseeing operations from Mar-a-Lago on March 1.

Trump said, `We want to be involved in the process of choosing the person who is going to lead Iran into the future`, comparing it to Venezuela and rejecting Mojtaba Khamenei to avoid another war within five years.

Iran's military response continues despite strikes that have killed over 1,000 civilians, while Iranian Kurdish militias have consulted with the United States and Trump said the Strait of Hormuz will remain open.

President Donald Trump said Thursday he must have a say in Iran's next leader, contradicting U.S. administration officials who deny regime change goals and signaling deeper involvement as the U.S.-led campaign continues.
title: Trump says he must be involved in selecting Iran's next leader

bias: Left outlets frame the roughly $200–$220M DHS ad campaign as cronyist waste—foregrounding "handpicked," "no‑bid," an alleged $143M subcontract and an "8‑day‑old company," using verbs like "grilled" and "battered" to convey moral outrage—while right outlets foreground DHS efficacy claims , casting scrutiny as partisan attack with rhetoric such as "savages" or "BRUTALLY GRILLS" and triumphant tone.
Center coverage is more measured—reporting skepticism and Noem’s defense and de‑emphasizing efficacy and firing anecdotes.
All agree on Senate questioning, spending scale, and contractor ties, exposing deeper corruption-versus-enforcement divides.
summary: This past week, NBC News reported that Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem handpicked contractors for an ICE recruitment campaign, according to internal communications and three administration officials, while a DHS spokesperson said decisions were made by the ICE Director’s office.

To meet recruitment targets, the campaign prioritized speed over an open bidding process, as DHS invoked a national emergency allowing avoidance of competitive bidding to hire 10,000 new ICE officers.

Federal records list People Who Think and Safe America Media, LLC as primary awardees, with Safe America Media incorporated days before winning $143 million and The Strategy Group subcontracted $226,000.

At recent hearings, lawmakers from both parties pressed Noem about the contracts, and President Donald Trump announced she will leave office March 31, with Markwayne Mullin, R-Okla., named as replacement.

Transparency concerns grew because subcontractors are not required to be disclosed, complicating oversight, and Sen. John Kennedy warned `It troubles me` that a fifth to a quarter-billion dollars was spent amid budget fights.
title: Noem Bypassed Bidding on $100M ICE Contracts, NBC Reports

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the rapprochement as driven by resource-seeking motives, using critical language like "pushes for minerals access" and "major/major shift" to spotlight U.S. economic interests, while right-leaning pieces celebrate it as a political triumph—calling it a "historical shift," praising "cut the red tape," and invoking "captured/ousted" to dramatize Maduro’s removal and claim "Trump’s foreign policy is working.
Rhetorically, the left adopts skeptical, accusatory tones; the right uses triumphalist, legitimizing diction.
The center de-emphasizes partisanship and highlights China competition.
Both sides agree on restored ties, Burgum’s visit, and links to stability, recovery and investment, reflecting deeper ideological and geopolitical fault lines.
summary: The United States and Venezuela's interim authorities have agreed to re-establish diplomatic and consular relations.
US Secretary of the Interior Doug Burgum said the intervention in Venezuela before Iran was a "brilliant strategic move" that would help ensure "oil will flow to America".
Venezuela's interim president Delcy Rodríguez and Burgum said the two countries would work together to develop mining in the resource-rich country.
title: US and Venezuela Agree to Resume Diplomatic Ties After Maduro Capture

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the incident as a failure of military oversight, using the Defense Secretary’s acknowledgment to underscore potential fault.
Conversely, right-leaning perspectives emphasize a "security-first" approach, focusing on the investigation to prevent regional escalation.
Both sides utilize emotive language like "scores of children" and "likely responsibility" to describe the tragedy.

While the left employs phrases like "points to" to suggest culpability, center-leaning reports provide unique context by highlighting the Pentagon’s denial and the 150 casualties—an "apparent strike" de-emphasized elsewhere.
All sides agree on the event's severity, reflecting shared concern over civilian harm amidst geopolitical tensions.
summary: On Saturday, U.S. military investigators said U.S. forces are likely responsible for an apparent strike on a girls' school in Minab, southern Iran, during coordinated attacks.

Coordinated U.S.-Israeli operations left differing target sets, with Israeli forces striking missile launch sites in western Iran and United States forces hitting southern naval targets, while the U.N. human rights office on Tuesday called for an investigation.

Military officials acknowledged the probe is ongoing, with Reuters unable to determine evidence supporting the assessment, while the Pentagon referred questions to U.S. Central Command, calling comment inappropriate.

On Tuesday, state television aired girls' funeral images showing small coffins draped with Iranian flags, while Ali Bahreini, Iran's ambassador to the U.N. in Geneva, said the strike killed 150 students, a figure Reuters could not confirm.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said `While the Department `of War is currently investigating this matter, the Iranian regime targets civilians and children, not the United States of America`, noting a U.S. role would rank among worst civilian casualties in decades.
title: US investigation points to likely US responsibility in Iran school strike, sources say

summary: This week, Beyond The Plant Protein Co. updated its public name and channels, dropping 'Meat' and now using 'Beyond' on its website, social media channels, and packaging.

Facing weak retail demand, Beyond has seen falling sales as its net revenue dropped 14% in the first nine months of 2025 and shares have traded below $1 this year, Chris Costagli said scrutiny hurt plant-based brands.

In January, Beyond launched Beyond Immerse and is testing new items online via Beyond Test Kitchen, while last summer it introduced Beyond Ground with four ingredients and plans a protein bar this summer.
Ethan Brown said the rebrand lets the company refocus on plant-sourced foods, adding Beyond will innovate plant-forward products like chickpea sausages and faba bean strips and put them in stores.
The broader plant-based sector is shifting toward drinks and snacks as consumer demand for protein soars, with Eat Just, Impossible Foods and Silk launching protein drinks and bars.
title: Beyond Meat drops the ‘Meat’ from its name as it pivots to plant-based drinks and snacks

bias: Left coverage frames bond yields as having "marched higher" after oil linked to the Iran conflict, offering a brief, trend-focused account that de-emphasized prior-week and year-ago comparisons and did not name the mortgage-data provider; center pieces call the oil move a "spike," label the rise a "modest increase" and give explicit attribution and numbers; right coverage calls the oil move a "surge," highlights the "benchmark 30‑year fixed-rate" and notes it "climbed slightly" to about 6%—all agree the rate rose to ~6%, ended a three-week slide, and tied the move to bond yields and oil.
summary: The average 30-year fixed mortgage rate ticked up to 6% from 5.98% last week, ending a three-week slide.
The modest increase follows a spike in oil prices due to the war with Iran, putting upward pressure on inflation and bond yields.
While mortgage rates have been trending lower in recent months, home sales remained stuck last year at 30-year lows.
title: Average US long-term mortgage rate ticks up to 6%, ending a three-week slide

bias: Left-Leaning pieces foreground human tragedy with emotive phrasing—repeating terms like "missing daughter," "shallow graves," and "mother of two girls" to personalize loss—while right-leaning coverage is de-emphasized in the analysis, leaving its counter-narrative unclear; center outlets emphasize legal labels such as "aggravated murder," use "accused," and include victims' ages .
The pivotal divide is familial, emotional framing versus legalistic specificity , yet all perspectives converge on police accounts, the suitcase discovery, and that two girls were killed.
summary: On Monday, officers recovered two bodies in suitcases near Ginn Academy in Cleveland's South Collinwood neighborhood; Aliyah Henderson, 28-year-old woman, was arrested Wednesday and charged with two counts of aggravated murder, with a court appearance Friday at Cleveland Municipal Court.
A dog walker alerted police after finding a suitcase in a field, leading detectives to use `intelligence-led policing strategies` to secure a search warrant, police said.
The Cuyahoga County Medical Examiner's Office identified the victims as 10-year-old Amor Wilson and 8-year-old Mila Chatman, confirming via preliminary DNA testing they were half-sisters.
DeShaun Chatman said he had been searching for his daughter Mila for five years before learning late Wednesday she was dead, and he sought emergency custody five times without success.
Police say the investigation remains active and the cause of death has not been released, and anyone with information should call the Cleveland Police Homicide Unit at 216-623-5464.
title: Mother of 2 girls found in shallow graves in Cleveland charged with murder

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds accusations—using words like "lied" and phrases such as "deflating Twitter stock" to frame corporate culpability and market harm—while right-leaning pieces emphasize Musk's rebuttal, stressing "denied," "were not," and his "high-profile" stature to portray his testimony as exculpatory; center reporting is more likely to quote his self-critique and note the roughly $44 billion deal.

The pivotal dividing lines are Musk's bot-accounts claim and his intent to lower the price—de-emphasized on the right and amplified on the left—yet all agree he testified and plaintiffs allege misleading statements.
summary: On Wednesday, Elon Musk took the stand in a San Francisco federal trial where shareholders accuse him of misleading statements before his 2022 purchase of Twitter for $44 billion.
Elon Musk's tweets about bots included claims nearly 20% of Twitter accounts were `fake`, causing Twitter's stock to fall nearly 10% on May 13.

Plaintiffs' lawyer Aaron Arnzen argued Musk waived due diligence and mounted a spectacle to lower the stock, while Musk told jurors `If this was a trial on whether I've made stupid tweets, I'd say I'm guilty`.
The lawsuit claims Musk violated federal securities laws by making false public statements, with shareholders seeking billions in damages if the jury rules in their favor.
Beyond this case, Musk is scheduled for an Oakland trial next month and has testified about a Tesla $420-per-share deal years ago, along with disputes involving OpenAI.
title: Elon Musk defends tweets in lawsuit alleging they caused Twitter stock to fall before acquisition

bias: Left outlets frame Obama's intervention as affirmative mobilization, using verbs like "threw his support" and emphasizing goals to "flip four Republican-held US House seats"; right outlets frame it as partisan opportunism, highlighting phrases such as "could help Dems gain 4 seats" and using shorthand "Dems" to signal skepticism.

Rhetorically, the left favors urgent, advocacy language while the right spotlights outcome-oriented, accusatory wording; pivotal divides include whether the change remedies mid‑decade GOP maps or constitutes a power grab, and the legal framing , which left de-emphasizes.
Both sides stress the ballot timing and stakes.
summary: On Thursday, Former President Barack Obama endorsed Democrats' redistricting effort in Virginia after the state Supreme Court cleared the way for the April 21 election.

To enable the change, the amendment would let the General Assembly redraw congressional maps until after the 2030 Census, allowing Democratic map-drawers to target Republican-held U.S. House seats by snaking lines from heavily Democratic suburbs around Washington, D.C.

Addressing voters directly, Obama urged Virginians to support the referendum, saying `In April, Virginians can respond by making sure your voting power is not diminished by what Republicans are doing in other states.`
A statewide vote on April 21 will decide if Virginia voters approve the map, with early voting starting March 6 and state Supreme Court approval required for it to take effect.

This coast-to-coast push has produced nine GOP-friendly seats and six seats favoring Democrats, part of a nationwide mid-decade redistricting effort that began last year in Texas at President Donald Trump's behest.
title: Obama urges Virginians to vote for redistricting measure
US interior secretary says Venezuela is offering security assurances to foreign mining companies

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes partisan motives and accountability, using charged tags like "Trump loyalist," "no prosecutorial experience," and "failed cases," naming targets to cast the prosecutions as politically driven, while center coverage frames the story as procedural, favoring neutral constructions like "investigated" and phrases such as "controversial prosecutions" and noting career prosecutors' objections; right-leaning treatment is de-emphasized in the materials provided.

Key dividing lines—labels of loyalty and inexperience, naming specific targets, and whether outcomes are "failed" versus merely "controversial"—draw starkly different emotional tones, though all agree a Florida Bar probe and prosecutor objections exist.
summary: On Thursday, The New York Times reported Florida's bar association opened an investigation after a letter last month indicated an inquiry into Lindsey Halligan, who asked to see the letter.

A judge in the Eastern District of Virginia ruled her appointment unlawful, dismissing her cases against James Comey and Letitia James, after she was appointed by President Donald Trump to pursue criminal cases, drawing scrutiny.

A letter to the Campaign for Accountability shows Florida's bar association can open investigations, send recommendations to a grievance committee and state courts, and that the process is rigorous and often takes years.
The potential outcome is disbarment, and the investigation could lead to that, though the process is rigorous and often takes years, according to sources.
Amid calls for accountability, observers note the move could signal a wake-up call for the Trump administration and deter attorneys considering DOJ jobs replacing many who quit last year.
title: Lindsey Halligan being investigated by Florida Bar

bias: Left coverage foregrounds human cost—framing the governor’s backing "despite" soldiers' "sacrifice" and using "retaliatory drone strike" to question escalation—while right coverage valorizes the fallen, saying she "lamented" losses, stressing procedural updates like "notified/families notified" and wounded en route to Germany, and presenting her "I believe in the mission right now" line as resolute.
Center pieces strike a solemn, community tone .
Flashpoints are the strike’s characterization and the governor’s quote; both sides agree on the deaths, condolences, flags at half-staff, but diverge along pro-security versus anti‑war priorities.
summary: On Thursday, March 5, 2026, Gov. Kim Reynolds ordered flags across Iowa to be flown at half-staff for two soldiers killed in Kuwait.

Both soldiers were assigned to the 103rd Sustainment Command, U.S. Army Reserve unit based in Des Moines and stationed at the logistics base in Port Shuaiba, Kuwait, amid escalating hostilities after the U.S. and Israel launched a military campaign against Iran one day earlier.

O'Brien's service record shows he was promoted to major in August 2024 and served nearly 15 years in the Army Reserve, while Coady was posthumously promoted to sergeant and studied cybersecurity at Drake University, Des Moines.

Flags will be lowered immediately and remain at half-staff until sunset on the final day of interment at the State Capitol Building, Capitol Complex, and all public buildings statewide; funeral arrangements are pending as the soldiers' bodies are returned.

The other slain soldiers include Sgt. Class Nicole Amor, Capt. Cody Khork, Sgt. Class Noah Tietjens and Chief Warrant Officer 3 Robert Marzan, and Senator Joni Ernst said `the nation owes the fallen an incredible debt of gratitude`.
title: Iowa governor believes in US war with Iran despite ‘sacrifice’ by state soldiers who died

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds the TikTok motive and uses moralizing, sensational phrasing—short tags like "Gets Prison For Setting Fire" and repeated use of "allegedly"—to frame the act as culture-war–linked political violence; notably, a "TikTok arsonist" label appears in some center headlines while variants surface elsewhere.
Right-Leaning coverage highlights partisan identity and foregrounds the judicial outcome, stressing the seven-year sentence and court details.
Center reporting uniquely adds post-sentencing supervision/no-contact conditions while sometimes using vivid phrases like "TikTok-inspired" and "fiery fury.
All sides agree on arson, the seven-year term, and a TikTok-linked motive, exposing a culture‑war versus law‑and‑order split.
summary: On Thursday, Caiden Stachowicz, 20, of Menasha, was sentenced to seven years in prison in Fond du Lac County for arson at Rep. Glenn Grothman's office.
Stachowicz told investigators he acted out of anger over TikTok policy, saying he opposed the federal push to force ByteDance to sell TikTok and felt his rights were violated.
Court records show he poured gasoline on an electrical box and around the building, then lit a match and watched it burn, according to the complaint.

Fond du Lac County Circuit Judge Tricia Walker ordered seven years of extended supervision and no contact with Rep. Glenn Grothman, with prosecutors saying `we hope this sentence acts as a deterrent to others`.
In January this year, TikTok finalized a deal to create an American entity, a platform used by more than 200 million Americans, signing agreements with Oracle, Silver Lake and MGX.
title: Wisconsin Man Who Set Fire to Grothman's Office Gets 7 Years in Prison

summary: On Friday, Feb. 27 the House Judiciary, Rules and Administration Committee unanimously advanced House Bill 696, which would expand Idaho's legal definition of sexual contact, sending it to the full House.

Spurred by InvestigateWest's investigation, lawmakers seek changes after reporting exposed sexual-abuse allegations against women's prison staff and Idaho's 1993 'sexual contact with a prisoner' law omits kissing and groping.
At last week's hearing, survivor Andrea Weiskircher said she accused five prison workers, and three other Idaho women urged lawmakers to pass the bill, including Audrey Numbers.

Supporters say the change could lead to more investigations and prosecutions, with Tina Transue, Department of Correction government relations advisor, saying `we're excited that it gives us more teeth`; if passed, the bill still must clear the Senate and the governor.

Erickson said the proposal alone is insufficient and signaled next year’s internal investigations reforms, while Idaho State Police indicated they will ramp up efforts against prison staff misconduct.
title: Idaho lawmakers move to expand ‘sexual contact’ definition to hold prison staff accountable

bias: Left outlets frame the incident as a civil‑liberties and security risk—highlighting a congressional notification that the culprit used "sophisticated techniques" and stressing exposure of "surveillance" or wiretap systems—while right outlets de‑emphasize that congressional detail, framing it as institutional vulnerability with words like "sensitive," "internal," "suspicious" to imply negligence.
Left uses alarmed, systemic language; right stresses breach and competence narratives.
Center is source‑attributive and reassuring, foregrounding the FBI 's "identified and addressed" claim and occasional "hack" vs "suspicious" phrasing.
All agree an investigation is underway and attribution is unresolved, reflecting ideological divides over state power, privacy, and institutional trust.
summary: The FBI identified and addressed "suspicious activities" on its networks related to wiretaps and intelligence surveillance.
The FBI did not provide details on the nature of the activities or when they were identified.
U.S. government networks are routinely targeted by hackers, with recent incidents at the Congressional Budget Office and federal courts.
title: FBI Investigating ‘Suspicious’ Cyber Activities on Critical Surveillance Network

bias: Left-Leaning coverage largely frames the story as a legal, procedural development—repeating that defense attorneys "seek his release" and de-emphasizing deposition/timeline detail—while right-leaning pieces foreground an exculpatory narrative, using words like "clears," "should be released," and "revelations" to stress due‑process and skepticism of prosecutors.
Center outlets emphasize the firefighter deposition and even use "demands," adopting a more urgent, investigative tone.

Pivotal divides hinge on the deposition/timeline and lack of a prosecutors' response; all sides agree a news conference occurred, charges exist, and defenders claim "new evidence," reflecting deeper splits over individual liberty versus institutional accountability.
summary: On March 5, defense attorney Haney demanded the immediate release of Rinderknecht after a firefighter's deposition said hot spots remained on Jan. 2.

Haney says deposition and civil testimony contradict the government's 'holdover fire' narrative and prosecutors' claim of a Jan. 2, 2025 underground ignition, with some hotspots still smoldering then.
In a sworn deposition, LAFD firefighter Scott Pike said he noticed about five smoking areas and kicked a smoldering spot to expose `red-hot like coals`.

Two judges previously denied bond, citing concerns about his mental health, family, and a firearm, while Haney questions ongoing detention based on newly disclosed evidence not available at indictment.

The Palisades fire, which began Jan. 7, 2025, killed 12 people and destroyed more than 7,000 structures, and Jaime Moore, Fire Department Chief, has commissioned an independent report amid homeowners' civil lawsuit.
title: Attorney demands release of man accused of starting Palisades fire

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the March 16 talks as substantive—highlighting Marcelo Ebrard’s Twitter announcement and agendas like "rules of origin," and portraying the USMCA as having "helped keep some of their commerce steady" despite a "constantly changing tariff policy.
Right-Leaning pieces use concise, procedural language, foreground SE and USTR as sources and de-emphasize technical topics, producing a plainer tone.
Pivotal divides are emphasis on negotiation content versus process and attribution of the announcement; center outlets repeat routine timing.
Both agree talks start March 16 and tie them to a scheduled USMCA review, reflecting ideological splits over regulation versus procedure.
summary: Mexico and the U.S. said Thursday they will start talks March 16 as part of the USMCA review, the U.S. Trade Representative's office confirmed.

Ahead of a scheduled review later this year, U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer and Mexican Secretary of Economy Marcelo Ebrard instructed negotiators to scope measures ensuring the USMCA benefits the parties and reduces outside dependence.
After initial talks, negotiators will meet regularly as part of the joint review, addressing rules of origin, supply chains, and tariffs including 25% on medium- and heavy-duty trucks.
With a July 1 deadline to notify Congress, the administration faces near-term pressure as the USMCA shields many Mexican goods from President Donald Trump 's tariffs.
The USMCA, enacted in 2020, frames a longer history of North American trade integration, while Mexico and Canada hold talks to strengthen cooperation ahead of this year’s review.
title: Mexico and US will start talks March 16 on reviewing their free trade agreement

bias: Left-Leaning pieces emphasize moral heroism and sacrifice—repeating the label "voting rights organizer" and highlighting "risky groundwork"—while right-leaning coverage is terser, crediting him with having "laid the foundations" but de-emphasizing broader legislative framing; center reporting frames him as the tactical "advance man" and explicitly links his work to the "Voting Rights Act of 1965.
Rhetorically, the left uses honorific, emotive repetition, the right prefers concise attribution, and the pivotal dividing line is whether coverage ties Selma to national legislation.
All sides agree on his death, age and cause , revealing shared respect amid ideological framing differences.
summary: Bernard LaFayette, civil rights organizer, died Thursday morning of a heart attack, his son Bernard LaFayette, III, said.
As director of the Alabama Voter Registration Campaign, Bernard LaFayette led advance work in Selma long ago that helped set the stage for the Voting Rights Act of 1965.
He was beaten outside his home, a neighbor with a rifle was called, and LaFayette later asked him not to shoot, surviving an assassination attempt on the night Medgar Evers was murdered.

DeMark Liggins, president of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference, said LaFayette's legacy lives in thousands he helped, with Chicago organizing credited for tenant protections and health efforts.
LaFayette also coordinated national campaigns and served in university leadership roles, including director of Peace and Justice in Latin America and at the University of Rhode Island.
title: Bernard LaFayette, Selma voting rights organizer, has died

summary: On March 5, 2026, a Waukesha County Circuit Court judge sentenced Nikita Casap to life in prison with no parole after his January guilty plea to two counts of first-degree intentional homicide.

Prosecutors say federal court filings describe a conspiracy aiming to start a 'political revolution,' with Casap's killings part of that effort to fund and carry out an assassination plot against President Donald Trump .
After the killings, he stole passports, the family car, and $14,000, then was arrested in Kansas on Feb. 28 after four days on the run.
Judge Ralph Ramirez denied parole eligibility after prosecutors said Casap is too dangerous while defense attorney Paul Rifelj asked for parole after at least 20 years.

Investigators uncovered a self-described 'Accelerate the Collapse' manifesto, extremist imagery, and messages show Casap communicated with a Russian-speaking contact while planning to flee to Ukraine and sent $8,700 in bitcoin to a scammer.
title: Wisconsin man who killed his parents to fund Trump assassination attempt gets life in prison

bias: Left coverage emphasizes relief—using 'welcomed all‑clear' and 'good news' and highlighting the asteroid’s ~60‑meter size and the revised 13,200‑mile miss from an earlier ~4.3% estimate—while right coverage is terse and absolutist, using 'no chance,' 'won't,' and 'slam' to deliver a brisk all‑clear without policy context.

Center pieces foreground procedural links and quote prior sensational labels like 'city killer.' Key dividing lines are NASA budget tie‑ins, asteroid size, and whether Earth risk was separately addressed; all sides, however, accept NASA’s updated zero‑chance assessment.
summary: Thursday's NASA statement from Cape Canaveral declared there is now zero chance that asteroid 2024 YR will crash into the moon in 2032.
James Webb observations on Feb. 18 and Feb. 26 showed NASA’s Center for Near-Earth Object Studies at JPL refined 2024 YR4's trajectory after earlier analyses put lunar-impact odds at 4.3%.
Discovered in late 2024 by ATLAS, Asteroid 2024 YR4 measures about 200 feet and will pass 13,200 miles from the lunar surface, with Webb observing it faintly since spring 2025.
NASA concluded the asteroid poses no significant impact risk to Earth on Dec. 22, 2032, or through the next century, and agencies plan continued observation including JWST in 2028.
Looking ahead, experts warned an impact could equal about 500 Hiroshima bombs and might have been visible from Earth or created meteor showers, while the rock will miss Earth six years from now.
title: No Chance Asteroid Will Slam Into the Moon in 2032, NASA Says

summary: A U.S. submarine struck the Iranian frigate IRIS Dena in international waters off Sri Lanka , with a DoD video showing the torpedo hit.
A Feb. 17 Indian navy post showed the Iranian frigate at sea during MILAN 2026 in Visakhapatnam, which included 74 countries, and the ship had been `a guest` of India's navy.
Sri Lanka's navy recovered 87 bodies and rescued 32 sailors after responding to a distress signal, and Iranian officials said the IRIS Dena had `almost 130` crew.
On Thursday, another Iranian warship entered Sri Lanka's exclusive economic zone and Sri Lanka's navy began transferring 208 sailors from the IRIS Bushehr to Colombo.

The sinking has prompted debate in India about maritime security; Indian opposition leaders criticized the silence while Abbas Araghchi called it `an atrocity at sea` and warned the U.S. `will come to bitterly regret` it.
title: Iranian warship sunk by the US was sailing home after taking part in an exhibition hosted by India

bias: Left-Leaning pieces frame the poll as a moral indictment—using words like "staggering," "catastrophic," and sarcastic asides such as "Can't Imagine Why," spotlighting alleged abuses and noting one-in-four Republicans backing abolition to treat 50% as a breakthrough; center coverage stresses numbers and trend language and links the shift to heightened enforcement while de-emphasizing abuse allegations; right-leaning coverage is de-emphasized in these analyses; both sides cite the YouGov/Economist 50% finding and tie it to enforcement, revealing a divide between moral outrage and data-focused framing.
summary: The YouGov/The Economist poll found 50% support for abolishing ICE, a first in their series, among 1,515 U.S. adults.

Months of intensified enforcement under the Trump administration and fatal January shootings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti fueled opposition as the Department of Homeland Security has been partially shut down since February 13 over ICE funding.
Demographic splits show Democrats back abolition at 77%, independents at 52%, and 23% of Republicans support ending ICE, while 58% say ICE uses excessive force, up from 51%.
Republican-Controlled U.S. Congress makes abolishing ICE unlikely this year, but the shift may influence Democratic lawmakers and campaign strategists ahead of the midterm elections.
Polls show rising distrust of ICE with only one-quarter of Americans confident and 68% demanding investigation into Renee Good and Alex Pretti shootings.
title: Support for Abolishing ICE Hits Record High: Economist/YouGov Poll

bias: Left outlets frame the story as evidence that a "pardoned by Trump" Jan. 6 rioter—a 45‑year‑old handyman convicted of molesting two children and given a life sentence—illustrates reckless clemency, emphasizing links to broader post‑pardon criminality and victim statements; right outlets foreground law‑and‑order language—calling him a "predator," stressing the heinous crimes and punitive outcome—using local, punitive rhetoric; center coverage sticks to chronology and terms like "sweeping act of clemency.
The key dividing line is whether pardons imply political responsibility or merely precede unrelated crimes; all agree on the conviction and sentence.
summary: On Thursday, Andrew Paul Johnson, a pardoned Jan. 6 defendant from Brooksville, was sentenced to life after a jury found him guilty earlier this year of molestation and transmitting harmful material to a minor.
Johnson met the boy's mother at a political rally in 2023 and later stayed in their home as a handyman, using gifts and promises of restitution to groom the boy and a 12-year-old girl.
Investigators recovered explicit messages on Discord and a phone Johnson had given a victim, and both victims testified about repeated abuse, with one saying `He said not to tell anybody`.

House Democrats introduced legislation to block federal payouts to former Jan. 6 defendants, while Congressman Jamie Raskin said the case shows pardons make Americans less safe and `They think they're untouchable`.
The Justice Department paid nearly five million to Ashli Babbitt's family, fueling debate on taxpayer payouts as the political right portrays Jan. 6 defendants as 'victims.
title: Man pardoned for Jan. 6 Capitol riot receives life sentence in Florida child molestation case

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes collective possibility—using inclusive frames like 'We' and instructional cues , highlighting 'recycling human waste' and casual terms such as 'moon dirt' and 'hummus' to normalize lunar agriculture and tout seed production as a milestone.

Right-Leaning pieces frame the result as incremental, invoking pop-culture , poetic minimizers like 'one small sprout,' and cautionary phrases such as 'safety and nutritional verification are still a challenge' to stress practical hurdles.
Center outlets call it a 'significant step.' Key dividing lines are waste-recycling claims, edibility/safety, and technical detail; all agree it advances lunar-food research tied to Artemis.
summary: Researchers from The University of Texas at Austin successfully harvested chickpeas grown in simulated 'moon dirt' or lunar regolith.
To maintain root hydration in the poor soil structure, a cotton wick-based irrigation system delivered water directly to the chickpea root zone.
While up to 75% moon dirt produced harvestable chickpeas, higher percentages caused plant stress and death, and the nutritional content and safety still need to be determined.
title: Scientists Successfully Harvest Chickpeas From 'Moon Dirt'

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes animal‑welfare outrage—using terms like "cruel treatment," "hurt," and noting an apology—to moralize culpability, while right-leaning pieces amplify sensational, punitive tones with words such as "torture," "notorious," and broad attention.
A pivotal dividing line is the taped‑mouth detail ; left pieces more often foreground local identifiers like Sublette County.
Center outlets adopt procedural phrasing and include age.
Both sides, however, report the guilty plea, the prosecutor's felony‑cruelty characterization, and the defendant's remorse, linking differences to wider cultural priorities.
summary: On March 5, 2026, Cody Roberts, 44-year-old Wyoming man, changed his plea to guilty to a felony animal-cruelty charge and apologized during a hearing at the Sublette County Courthouse in Pinedale as Robert Piper, defense attorney, exited the courtroom.

The episode began Feb. 29, 2024, in Daniel when Roberts brought an injured wolf into the Green River Bar, leading to a $250 fine and a Wyoming grand jury indictment, with a plea agreement signed Feb. 17.
Video and photos showed the wolf with its muzzle taped with tape and lying barely moving while Roberts posed with a beer inside the Green River Bar.
Under the plea deal, Roberts faces a $1,000 fine and 18 months' supervised probation, including bans on alcohol, hunting, and firearm possession, and prompted over 5,000 emails to the judge's office.

The episode intensified scrutiny of Wyoming's predator-killing laws after photos circulated, dividing the community of 9,000 residents in Sublette County and prompting state social media pages to go dark amid backlash.
title: Wyoming man accused of taking wolf he hurt with snowmobile into bar changes plea to guilty
Hezbollah Warns Israeli Residents to Evacuate Towns Near Border

bias: Left-Leaning coverage "decries" the U.S.-Israeli missile attack as "arrogant," employing satirical language like "smarter than our president" and questioning the cost of "start an entire war.

Conversely, right-leaning outlets frame the action as a necessary "mission to destroy" "Iran missile, naval threats," using decisive terms like "attack" and highlighting Secretary Rubio's statements on military objectives while de-emphasizing regime change.
Center-Leaning reports neutrally present Rubio's comments on mitigating "oil price spikes" and the mission's scope.

While all acknowledge the strikes and the death of Iran's Supreme Leader, these narratives underscore fundamental ideological divides concerning intervention, leadership, and national security priorities.
summary: On Monday, Secretary of State Marco Rubio told reporters on Capitol Hill that Operation Epic Fury aims to eliminate Iran's short-range ballistic missiles and naval capabilities, saying `That is the clear objective of this mission`.
On February 28, coordinated U.S. and Israeli strikes began that targeted multiple Iranian sites as officials expected retaliation and sought to reduce U.S. casualties, Rubio said.

Reports from Iranian and international sources indicate six U.S. service members have died, 555 people were killed across Iran, and Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and four senior officials were killed in strikes.

Iran launched retaliatory strikes that targeted U.S. assets in the Gulf and Israel while Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait and Jordan also faced attacks, raising concern among Democrats, critics and some Republicans about the unclear mission timeline.
Rubio said the mission's goal is destruction of Iran's missile and naval capabilities, disputing that it is about regime change, according to the source.
title: Rubio says U.S. mission in Iran targets missile, naval threats

summary: On Monday, Israeli settlers killed two brothers in Qaryout, south of Nablus, the Palestinian Red Crescent reported; three others were injured by gunfire.

Since the Gaza war began, violence in the West Bank has surged, with the Wall and Settlement Resistance Commission recording around 4,723 cases of settler violence during 2025, and recent killings add to this trend.
Settlers fired live rounds and burned properties during the assault, while crowds tried to rescue victims and ambulances faced delays amid tear gas fired by Israeli forces.
Wounded were taken to Nablus hospital as Israel's military said it was `looking into` the reports, including a 15-year-old boy and three others.

The U.N. humanitarian office reported last year the highest daily average of settler attacks since 2006, while Palestinian residents and rights groups say Israeli authorities routinely fail to prosecute settlers, worsening impunity.
title: Israeli Settler Gunfire Kills Two Palestinian Brothers in West Bank

bias: Left-Leaning outlets emphasize the "intense" nature of the "bombardment," framing the "unrelenting campaign" through its destructive "material cost" and potential for escalation.
Conversely, right-leaning perspectives celebrate a "major hit," using triumphant language like "blown to smithereens" and "bursts into flames" to portray military dominance.
While the right highlights the systematic dismantling of nearly 200 targets as a strategic victory, the left includes casualty figures de-emphasized by the right.
Center-Leaning reports describe the navy as "decimated," prioritizing decisive action.
Both sides agree the WWII-sized vessel was struck, reflecting a shared focus on degrading Iranian capabilities.
summary: CENTCOM said Friday U.S. forces struck an Iranian drone carrier nearly the size of a World War II aircraft carrier, which is on fire, and have sunk over 30 Iranian navy vessels.
Operation Epic Fury began early Saturday and CENTCOM briefed reporters at MacDill Air Force Base, Tampa, Florida, about ramped-up capabilities.
CENTCOM added that strikes hit Iran's missile and space infrastructure, including its equivalent of Space Command, in the last 72 hours.
Adm. Brad Cooper said Iran's navy has been decimated and its drone capabilities damaged, while U.S. officials vowed the campaign will continue at full intensity with more firepower planned.

In Washington, officials say the campaign aims to weaken Iran's naval capabilities through sustained operations, amid expanding hostilities, despite the House narrowly rejecting a war powers resolution.
title: US military sets Iranian drone carrier ship on fire

bias: Left outlets frame the outreach as interventionist—calling it support for "insurgent efforts" and to "seize parts" of Iran with "extensive air cover"—while right outlets celebrate U.S. backing, using phrases like "throw his weight behind," "wonderful," "fighters," and "regime change" to legitimize an offensive; the left’s tone is alarmed and accusatory, the right’s triumphant and valorizing.
Key divides: reports of CIA talks and the White House denial versus center-cited warnings about Iranian public unity .
Both agree Trump spoke with Kurdish leaders, offered backing, and that escalation risks increase.
summary: President Donald Trump reportedly offered `extensive US air cover` during calls with Kurdish leaders, including Mustafa Hijri and Masoud Barzani, to support Kurdish groups in western Iran.

Amid heavy US‑Israeli strikes along the Iraq–Iran border, US officials say the outreach aims to stretch Iranian forces and create a northern buffer as the war entered its sixth day with more than 1,000 killed.

Formed in Iraqi Kurdistan, the coalition reportedly brings together groups that field thousands of fighters, with KDPI about 1,200, PAK about 1,000 and PJAK’s YRK believed to number between 1,000 and 3,000.

Following missile and drone attacks and debris near Erbil airport on March 4, 2026, Iranian military struck Kurdish targets with three missiles and 30 drones, while Nechirvan Barzani said the region `will not be part of conflicts`.
Past US‑Kurdish ties and the potential for troop deployment raise risks of a new front, as the US has armed and trained the YPG and Peshmerga, analysts said.
title: Trump offers US backing, including aircover, to Iranian Kurdish groups if they go on offensive: Washington Post

bias: Left outlets frame the Pentagon’s action as regulatory intervention—using terms like "at-risk" and warning of "dramatic consequences" while highlighting Anthropic ’s ethical stance and de-emphasizing alleged operational-use claims; right outlets stress national-security and political accountability—invoking "supply-chain risk," "following through with its threat" and calling it an "unprecedented move" tied to a deal turndown.
Center coverage foregrounds legal and business angles, using "no choice," "slapped," and "customers unaffected.
The pivotal split is political retaliation versus national-security rationale; all agree on the immediate designation and its limits on government use.
summary: On Friday, Dario Amodei, co-founder and CEO of Anthropic , said, `As we wrote on Friday, we do not believe this action is legally sound, and we see no choice but to challenge it in court.`

The dispute grew after the Defense Department demanded unfettered access to Claude for all lawful military purposes, while Anthropic sought assurances against use in autonomous weapons or mass surveillance.

The leaked internal memo prompted Amodei to apologize and said it was out-of-date, while defense vendors must certify they don't use Anthropic's models in Pentagon work, despite support for U.S. operations in Iran and a last year's $200 million contract.

President Donald Trump directed federal agencies to immediately cease all use of Anthropic's technology on Friday, while Amodei said Anthropic customers remain mostly unaffected and Palantir shares fell 2% on Thursday.
Anthropic plans to contest its supply-chain risk designation in federal court in Washington, while legal analysts note courts rarely second-guess national-security decisions.
title: Anthropic CEO says ‘no choice’ but to challenge Trump admin’s supply chain risk designation in court
